bibliographic_data.title: Shape and Reflectance Reconstruction in Uncontrolled Environments by Differentiable Rendering
bibliographic_data.abstract: Simultaneous reconstruction of geometry and reflectance in uncontrolled environments remains a challenging problem. In this paper, we propose an efficient method to reconstruct the scene’s 3D geometry and reflectance from multi-view photography using conventional hand-held cameras. Our method automatically builds a virtual scene in a differentiable rendering system that roughly matches the real world's scene parameters, optimized by minimizing photometric objectives alternatingly and stochastically. With the optimal scene parameters evaluated, photo-realistic novel views for various viewing angles and distances can then be generated by our approach. We present the results of captured scenes with complex geometry and various reflectance types. Our method also shows superior performance compared to state-of-the-art alternatives in novel view synthesis visually and quantitatively.

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper proposes a systematic scene parameter reconstruction method that jointly estimates 3D geometry, surface reflectance, specular coefficients, camera pose, and lighting condition using a differentiable inverse rendering framework.
core_content.key_methodology: The method uses a differentiable rendering system to optimize the virtual scene parameters iteratively by minimizing photometric objectives. It leverages a physically-based engine like Mitsuba2 for forward and backward propagation of gradients.
outcomes: Successfully reconstructs 3D geometry and reflectance from multi-view photography in uncontrolled environments.
Generates photo-realistic novel views with various viewing angles and distances.
core_content.full_text_markdown: The paper addresses the challenge of simultaneously reconstructing an object's 3D geometry and its surface reflectance from multiple images taken by conventional hand-held cameras. The authors propose a method that uses differentiable rendering to optimize scene parameters iteratively, ensuring photometric consistency between rendered images and real photographs. This approach enables the reconstruction of complex scenes in uncontrolled environments without requiring controlled lighting or specific camera setups. The method is demonstrated through various experiments showing its effectiveness compared to existing techniques.
